danger_signals.visual_form: Cartoon soldiers closing distance on isometric battlefield
danger_signals.meaning: Close combat about to initiate; immediate threat
Unit incapacitated; requires medic intervention or will be lost

danger_signals.visual_form: Gunner disabled for several seconds during attack
danger_signals.meaning: Loss of firepower capability; vulnerability window

danger_signals.visual_form: Flamethrower unit on top of another unit
danger_signals.meaning: High danger; difficult to survive this engagement

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy reduced to skeleton, ash pile, or remains
danger_signals.meaning: Unit permanently eliminated; medic cannot revive

danger_signals.visual_form: Bubbles instead of flame when Scorcher enters water

safety_signals.visual_form: Another soldier moving close to fallen unit and calling medic
safety_signals.meaning: Fallen soldier can be revived; unit recovery possible
Protection from certain weapon types (Launcher attacks can be blocked)

safety_signals.visual_form: Items visible on battlefield to pick up
safety_signals.meaning: Point accumulation; medic use replenishment at thresholds
Avoidance of close combat; ranged attack advantage

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Player 1 team soldiers vs. General Havoc team soldiers
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Capital city as objective location; dynamic campaign map shows territorial control

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Opposing team soldiers distinguished in multiplayer/campaign
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Capital city as opposing objective; decades of territorial war shown through campaign progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Isometric perspective view of cartoon soldiers in combat formation; real-time action on battlefield
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Soldiers move, attack, take cover; medics called; units fall and revive

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dynamic strategic map showing territorial control between Moronica and Viceria
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Series of battles fought over map; objectives shift based on territorial gains

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Soldier falls to ground (visual collapse animation)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Unit becomes immobile; cannot act until revived or battle ends; medic intervention required

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy reduced to skeleton (Launcher/Blaster) or ash pile (Scorcher); remains on battlefield
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Unit removed from game; no medic revival possible; team strength diminished

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: No visual indicator described; mechanical limit reached
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Fallen soldiers cannot be revived; must be replenished through point threshold achievements

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Capital city siege in campaign mode
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final objective requires capture of enemy capital; determines campaign winner

environmental_storytelling.example: Cartoon-style battlefield viewed from angled perspective communicates satirical tone and arcade nature of conflict
Territorial changes between Moronica and Viceria show progression of decades-long war and stakes of individual battles
Objective placement on map conveys political significance; capture determines overall victory
Point-yielding objects suggest looting/salvage from conflict; medic replenishment through threshold achievement ties resource scarcity to battle progression

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Medium-range attacks with high rate of fire; well-rounded tactical unit; can jam temporarily
Long-range, powerful, slow-firing; attacks blocked by cover; can cause instant death; can destroy bonus objectives
Low rate of fire; grenades arc over cover; damage multiple units; good range; can destroy objectives
Shortest range but highest threat; hits multiple soldiers; fastest-firing; excellent for flanking; deadly when on top of enemy unit
Weapon disabled; shoots bubbles instead of fire; tactical vulnerability
Heavy area damage; extremely slow rate of fire; second-shortest range; low tactical effectiveness; can destroy objectives; can cause instant death
2-unit team with different control system; greater individual unit control compared to standard 5-soldier teams
Immobile on ground; requires medic proximity and call to revive; cannot act until restored
Reduced to skeleton or ash; permanently removed from battlefield; cannot be revived by medic

notes: General Chaos uses visual class distinction (buzz cut, helmet, headband, dark glasses, eye patch/beard) to communicate unit roles through appearance. The satirical cartoon aesthetic communicates the humorous nature of the fictional conflict between brothers Chaos and Havoc. The game's visual communication relies heavily on unit proximity mechanics (close combat triggers) and status visualization (fallen soldiers, instant death effects). The two fictional nations (Moronica and Viceria) are distinguished primarily through campaign map control rather than distinct visual faction markers, though multiplayer/campaign mechanics imply opposing visual team representation not detailed in source.
